description: Figure 3 presents SEM micrographs illustrating the annealed microstructures of an Fe–8.03Mn–6.10Al–0.4C (wt%) low-density duplex steel processed via two distinct cooling paths after hot rolling. Panels (a–c) show air-cooled (AC) samples annealed at 800, 900, and 1000 °C (AC-A800, AC-A900, AC-A1000), respectively, while panels (d–f) display furnace-cooled (FC) counterparts (FC-A800, FC-A900, FC-A1000). The AC-annealed microstructures consist of austenite (γ) and needle-like α-ferrite embedded within the austenite bands, alongside δ-ferrite, with progressively coarser austenite grains as annealing temperature increases. In contrast, FC-annealed samples exhibit a banded morphology comprising δ-ferrite, prior austenite, and a small fraction of martensite (α′), plus fine equiaxed α-ferrite, but notably lack needle-like α-ferrite. The average austenite grain sizes in FC samples are substantially larger than those in AC samples at each temperature. These microstructural differences demonstrate that the initial cooling path critically governs phase morphology, grain refinement, and austenite stability, directly influencing the subsequent strength-ductility synergy.
